content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

The Nasarenko Advice Letter, No. 14-015, supersedes this Nolan Advice letter No. I-88-188 in
part, to the extent that it discusses with approval the conclusion in the Brown Letter.

June 17, 1988


Honorable Patrick Nolan
Member of the Assembly
State Capitol, Room 2114
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:     Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-88-188

Dear Assemblyman Nolan:

You have requested written advice regarding certain
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/. This
will confirm telephone advice rendered to your staff on June 1,
1988.

QUESTION

May you send out birth announcements, paid for by your
campaign committee, "Friends of Nolan," without complying with
the requirements for sender identification of mass mailings
sent by committees?

CONCLUSION

You should consult with the Attorney General to determine
whether such an expenditure by your committee would be
permitted under the "personal use" statute, Elections Code
Section 12400, et seq.

___________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to
Regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Honorable Patrick Nolan
June 17, 1988
Page -2-


FACTS

You are an elected state officer and, therefore, a
candidate within the meaning of the Act. (See Section 82007.)
You wish to send out announcements of the birth of your child.
The announcements will total more than 200 pieces of mail.
Your staff has advised that you wish to have the cost of
printing and mailing borne by your campaign committee, Friends
of Nolan.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires a mass mailing of 200 or more pieces of
substantially similar mail sent by a candidate or committee to
be identified with the name and address of the candidate or
committee. (Sections 82041.5 and 84305.)

As you have indicated in your letter, in the Brown Advice
Letter, No. A-83-296, the Commission exempted holiday greeting
cards from this requirement, provided that the cards make no
reference to the candidate's nomination or election matters.
This exemption was based on the fact that the mailings would be
identified as being sent by the candidate, not the committee.
We assume that the birth announcement would identify it
as coming from you, the candidate. However, it is also clearly
of a personal nature as opposed to a political nature.

This latter point raises a different issue, which was not
addressed in our previous advice letter. Elections Code
Section 12100, et seq., set forth the proper uses for campaign
funds. Under those provisions, "personal use" of campaign
funds is prohibited. This agency does not administer or
interpret the provisions of the Elections Code. We suggest
that you contact the Attorney General for an interpretation of
these sections as applied to campaign expenditures for sending
out birth announcements.

Once you have received advice from the Attorney General
regarding the permissibility of having your campaign committee
pay for such a mailing, please feel free to contact this agency
again regarding the sender identification issue. Should you
have questions regarding this letter, I may be reached at (916)
322-5901.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: Robert E. Leiggh
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

Honorable Patrick Nolan  
June 17, 1988

The Act requires a mass mailing of 200 or more pieces of substantially similar mail sent by a candidate or committee to be identified with the name and address of the candidate or committee. (Sections 82041.5 and 84305.)

As you have indicated in your letter, in the Brown Advice Letter, No. A-83-296, the Commission exempted holiday greeting cards from this requirement, provided that the cards make no reference to the candidate's nomination or election matters. This exemption was based on the fact that the mailings would be identified as being sent by the candidate, not the committee. This latter point raises a different issue, which was not addressed in our previous advice letter. Elections Code Section 12400, et seq., set forth the proper uses for campaign funds.
